title: BUS 340 — Information Systems Management
description: The full BUS 340 curriculum at NC State. Understand how organizations design, use, and manage information systems — from databases and enterprise software through cybersecurity, AI, and digital transformation.
subject: Information Systems

learn.explanation: An information system (IS) is a set of interrelated components that collect, process, store, and distribute information to support decision-making and control in an organization. The five core components are people, processes, data, hardware, and software/networks. Importantly, IS is broader than IT — IT refers to the technical infrastructure alone, while IS includes the human and organizational layer on top of it. A company's CRM system is IT; the people, workflows, training, and business decisions built around that CRM is an IS.
learn.example: Amazon's order fulfillment system is a classic IS. The hardware (servers, scanners) and software (order management platform) are the IT components. But the IS includes warehouse workers following picking processes, managers using dashboards to spot bottlenecks, and business rules that trigger automatic reorders when stock drops below a threshold. Remove the people and processes and you have IT infrastructure — not an information system.
keyPoints: An IS = people + processes + data + hardware + software working together toward an organizational goal
IT is a subset of IS — the technical components only. IS adds people and process to make technology useful
IS fails most often at the people and process layer, not the technical layer — adoption and alignment matter more than hardware

learn.explanation: Organizations use different IS types for different levels of decision-making. Transaction Processing Systems (TPS) handle daily operations — recording sales, processing payroll, logging inventory. Management Information Systems (MIS) aggregate TPS data into reports for middle managers. Decision Support Systems (DSS) help managers analyze options using models and data. Executive Information Systems (EIS) give senior leaders high-level dashboards and strategic summaries. Each level builds on the one below it — executives cannot make good strategic decisions without reliable operational data flowing up through TPS and MIS.
learn.example: A hotel chain uses a TPS to record every room booking and payment in real time. The MIS aggregates this into weekly occupancy reports by location for regional managers. A DSS lets the revenue manager model how different pricing strategies would affect bookings based on historical patterns. The EIS gives the CEO a single dashboard showing portfolio-wide occupancy, revenue per available room, and year-over-year trends across all 400 properties.
keyPoints: TPS: operational, high volume, structured transactions — the foundation of all organizational IS
MIS: aggregates TPS data into scheduled reports for middle management decisions
DSS and EIS serve higher-level decisions — less structured, more analytical, more strategic

learn.explanation: IS creates business value in three ways: operational efficiency (doing the same work faster and cheaper), decision quality (making better choices with better information), and competitive advantage (doing things rivals cannot easily replicate). The third is the most strategically important. When IS capabilities are rare, hard to imitate, and aligned with strategy — like Netflix's recommendation engine or Amazon's fulfillment network — they become sustained competitive advantages. Generic IS (using the same accounting software as every competitor) creates no competitive differentiation.
learn.example: Zara's IS is a case study in competitive advantage. Their system connects point-of-sale data from 2,000+ stores directly to design and production teams, enabling a two-week design-to-shelf cycle vs. the industry standard of six months. This IS capability — fast feedback loops between customer demand and production — is the core of Zara's business model. Competitors cannot replicate it quickly because it requires simultaneous changes to systems, supplier relationships, and organizational processes.
keyPoints: IS value = efficiency + decision quality + competitive advantage — the third is hardest to build and most durable
Competitive IS advantage requires rare, hard-to-imitate capabilities — not just better software, but better systems thinking
IS that every competitor uses equally creates no differentiation — strategic value comes from unique system-enabled capabilities
